coredata.dc:title: Development of paper-based DNA sensor for detection of O. tsutsugamushi using sustainable GQDs@AuNPs nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Kala, Deepak
Sharma, Tarun Kumar
Gupta, Shagun
Saini, Reena V.
Saini, Adesh K.
Alsanie, Walaa F.
Thakur, Vijay Kumar
Kaushal, Ankur
coredata.dc:description: The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was synthesized using potato starch and water by hydrothermal method and further used for reduction of tetracholoroauric acid to form graphene quantum dots-gold (GQDs@AuNPs) nanocomposite. The GQDs/GQDs@AuNPs were analyzed using FTIR, UV–Vis, Flourometry and HR-TEM. The synthesized GQDs@AuNPs were further used for fabrication of cost-effective screen-printed paper electrode (SPPE) based DNA sensor for the detection of O. tsutsugamushi using htrA gene specific 5′NH2 linked DNA probe. Modification of SPPE using GQDs@AuNPs nanocomposite and ssDNA probe was monitored using EIS, FTIR, FE-SEM and AFM. The sensor detection limit (LOD) was assessed as 0.002 ng/μl from the standard calibration curve with the correlation coefficient, R2 = 0.993. The sensitivity of the DNA sensor was calculated as 7700 μA/cm2/ng for ssGDNA of O. tsutsugamushi using cyclic voltammetry. The sensor validation was done using scrub typhus patient's blood DNA samples. The sensor showed good storage stability at 4 °C for six months with just a loss of 12% of the initial current values. The SPPE/DNA sensor developed is very specific, sensitive, stable and detects O. tsutsugamushi in less time.
coredata.dcterms:subject: GQDs
GQDs@AuNPs nanocomposite
Screen printed paper electrode
Scrub typhus
DNA sensor

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Pharmaceutical and personal care products (PPCPs) treatment and sensing by 2D carbon nanomaterials; challenges and perspectives, edited by Hassan Karimi-Maleh, Yasin Orooji and Mehmet Lutfi Yola

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots-gold (GQDs@AuNPs) nanocomposite
Immobilization of ssDNA probe onto the GQDs@AuNPs modified screen printed paper electrode and measurement of sensor response
Characterization of GQDs and GQDs@AuNPs nanocomposite
Measurement of DNA sensor response using cyclic voltammetry
Specificity, selectivity and stability of DNA sensor

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Department of Science and Technology, Ministry of Science and Technology, India

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The funding to carry out the proposed work was received from the Department of Science and Technology, (DST), Govt. of India (SP/YO/079/2017).The authors are thankful to Dr. Amisha Sharma, Head, Department of Microbiology, MMMC&H, Solan, H. P. for providing patient's DNA sample to carry out the research work. We would also like to thank the Central Instrument Research Facility, Amity University Haryana (UV?Vis, FTIR, and PL facility), Central Instrumentation Laboratory, Punjab University (HRTEM), and Materials research center, MNIT Jaipur (FE-SEM and AFM) for providing the characterization facilities. Walaa F. Alsanie acknowledges Taif University for support No. TURSP (2020/53).
The funding to carry out the proposed work was received from the Department of Science and Technology, ( DST ), Govt. of India ( SP/YO/079/2017 ).

The authors are thankful to Dr. Amisha Sharma, Head, Department of Microbiology, MMMC&H, Solan, H. P. for providing patient's DNA sample to carry out the research work. We would also like to thank the Central Instrument Research Facility, Amity University Haryana (UV–Vis, FTIR, and PL facility), Central Instrumentation Laboratory, Punjab University (HRTEM), and Materials research center, MNIT Jaipur (FE-SEM and AFM) for providing the characterization facilities. Walaa F. Alsanie acknowledges Taif University for support No. TURSP (2020/53 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of (a) GQDs and (b) GQDs@AuNPs nanocomposite using (A) UV–Vis spectra in a wavelength of 200–800 nm, (B) FTIR spectra at a frequency range 500–4000 cm-1, and (C) HRTEM images of GQDs@AuNPs nanocomposite with histogram to calculate the size distribution.

Characterization of SPPE/GQDs@AuNPs/DNA Probe using AFM at different phases of modification including A. blank screen-printed paper electrode (BSPPE), B. modification of SPPE using GQDs@AuNPs nanocomposite and, C. covalent attachment of ssDNA probe onto the GQDs@AuNPs/SPPE. The Figure A–C represents the 2D images of working electrode (W.E.) at different phases with roughness in decreasing order from A-B and increased in C. The Figure D–F shows the 3D images of the W.E. at different phases with the measurement of height.

The electron micrograph of (a) BSPPE, (b) GQDs@AuNPs/SPPE, and (c) SPPE/GQDs@AuNPs electrode covalently attached with ssDNA probe. The FE-SEM micrograph was captured to monitor the occurrence of DNA sensor fabrication steps.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy of DNA sensor at different fabrication phases including BSPPE (A), GQDs@AuNPs/SPPE (B), covalently attached DNA probe on SPPE/GQDs@AuNPs electrode (C), and its hybridization with complementary ssGDNA (D–E). The EIS study was performed using 2.5 mM potassium ferricyanide prepared in PBS buffer (pH 7.2) in frequency range of 0.1 Hz–105 Hz with the Edc = 0.15 V and Eac = 0.006 V. The inset (II) shows the Rct values of DNA sensor at different steps of electrode modifications (A–C) and hybridization events (D–E). The inset (III) shows the equivalent circuit of the DNA sensor.

Cyclic voltammetric (CV) measurements of DNA sensor response using 1 mM methylene blue in PBS buffer, pH 7.2. The CV of bare SPPE/GQDs@AuNPs (a), SPPE/GQDs@AuNPs covalently attached with 5′NH2 ssDNA probe (b), and after binding with the ssGDNA of O. tsutsugamushi (c–k) in a scan rate of 30 mV/sec. The inset (II) displays the standard calibration curve used in the study to assess the LOD of DNA sensor. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

The specificity study of DNA sensor after hybridization of ssDNA probe with the complementary ssGDNA of O. tsutsugamushi and ssGDNA of other bacterial species (E. coli, K. pneumoniae, S. aureus, and L. interrogans). The inset shows the relative Ip values (with respect to probe) of DNA sensor after hybridization with ssGDNA samples.

Validation of DNA sensor using 30 patient's blood GDNA sample, positive control (PCR confirmed O. tsutsugamushi GDNA sample) and negative control (h-GDNA). The sample A, B and C are the positive samples (12), negative samples (13) and TE buffer without GDNA respectively. The relative peak current with respect to the DNA probe after hybridization with patient's ssGDNA samples was used for its validation. All measurements were done using similar concentration of ssGDNA (20 ng/μl) by cyclic voltammetry using 1 mM methylene blue dye (PBS, pH 7.2). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
The Nucleotide sequences (cDNA and mismatch bases) used in selectivity.

head.ce:title: Development of paper-based DNA sensor for detection of O. tsutsugamushi using sustainable GQDs@AuNPs nanocomposite

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The graphene quantum dots (GQDs) was synthesized using potato starch and water by hydrothermal method and further used for reduction of tetracholoroauric acid to form graphene quantum dots-gold (GQDs@AuNPs) nanocomposite. The GQDs/GQDs@AuNPs were analyzed using FTIR, UV–Vis, Flourometry and HR-TEM. The synthesized GQDs@AuNPs were further used for fabrication of cost-effective screen-printed paper electrode (SPPE) based DNA sensor for the detection of O. tsutsugamushi using htrA gene specific 5′NH2 linked DNA probe. Modification of SPPE using GQDs@AuNPs nanocomposite and ssDNA probe was monitored using EIS, FTIR, FE-SEM and AFM. The sensor detection limit (LOD) was assessed as 0.002 ng/μl from the standard calibration curve with the correlation coefficient, R2 = 0.993. The sensitivity of the DNA sensor was calculated as 7700 μA/cm2/ng for ssGDNA of O. tsutsugamushi using cyclic voltammetry. The sensor validation was done using scrub typhus patient's blood DNA samples. The sensor showed good storage stability at 4 °C for six months with just a loss of 12% of the initial current values. The SPPE/DNA sensor developed is very specific, sensitive, stable and detects O. tsutsugamushi in less time.

Scheme 1. Schematic of SPCE/GQDs A/ssDNA (probe) based Electrochemical DNA sensor for the detection of scrub typhus.
Image 1

ce:section.ce:para: Scrub typhus is one among the world's most neglected or under-diagnosed diseases, that affects a million people every year mostly in the Asian Pacific region (Luce-Fedrow et al., 2018; Banerjee and Kulkarni, 2020; Kala et al., 2020a). It is originated from a bacterium known as Orientia tsutsugamushi (family Rickettsiaceae) and spread by the bite of its vector mite, Leptotrombidium deliense (Kala et al., 2020b). The disease was initially endemic to a traditional area known as tsutsugamushi triangle, but later out-spread to non-endemic regions with evolution of new strains (Banerjee and Kulkarni, 2020; Kala et al., 2020b). The spread of the disease in several countries raised the concern of its medical management (Gaba et al., 2021). The nonspecific clinical manifestations and antigenic variations among its serotypes emerged as a major obstacle in its diagnosis and prognosis triggering a need for advanced diagnosis methods with better sensitivity, specificity and ability to detect the infection at initial stages. Currently available serological methods for the detection of scrub typhus include Weil-Felix, ICT, ELISA, and IFA (Kannan et al., 2020). The Weil-felix test was used initially for screening of scrub typhus but not in current clinical practice due to its poor specificity and sensitivity (Banerjee and Kulkarni, 2020; Kala et al., 2020b). Among all serological methods, IFA is considered as a gold standard for the detectionon of scrub typhus (Peter et al., 2015). However, even this cannot detect the disease at the initial stages as the antibodies are raised 7–14 days post-infection (Banerjee and Kulkarni, 2020). The molecular diagnosis methods include PCR (nested PCR, qPCR, Real time PCR) and LAMP (loop mediated isothermal amplification) assay. PCR based diagnosis method targets genetic markers such as tsa, htrA, GroEL, and 16s rRNA gene for detection of O. tsutsugamushi (Kala et al., 2020a, b; Kala et al., 2020b). The htrA (high temperature requirement A) gene encodes a 47 kDa antigenic protein on the bacterial surface and shows homology with the genes of high-temperature requirement (HtrA) family of stress response proteins that have both chaperone and endoprotease activities (Jiang et al., 2004). It is conserved among the strains of O. tsutsugamushi identified from different countries and is considered one of the prominent tools for the molecular diagnosis of scrub typhus. (Jiang et al., 2013; Yáñez-Sedeño et al., 2020; Saravanan et al., 2020). The Molecular diagnosis methods such as qPCR show superior sensitivity and specificity for diagnosis of scrub typhus at early stages with more accuracy (Kannan et al., 2020). However, the method offers various limitations of being time-consuming, costly, requirement of expensive reagents, and sophisticated instrumentation. The occurrence of the disease in rural areas triggered the need of point of care diagnosis facilities that can be used in resource limited settings. The biosensors are the need of the hour due to their ability of onsite detection with higher sensitivity and specificity. The electrochemical DNA sensors benefit from a range of distinct chemistries, but one common advantage for all is the nanoscale interaction in between the analyte in solution and biotransducer surface (Drummond et al., 2003). The electrochemical DNA sensor offers several advantages over other molecular assays including portability, lower power consumption, detection of analyte in complex body fluids directly on the basis of nucleic acid hybridization principal without any requirement of enzyme-based target amplification like in PCR. Nanomaterials are playing a major role in development of various sensors used for disease diagnosis, gases detection and heavy metals detection etc. (Seid et al., 2022; Berkani et al., 2022; Doan et al., 2021; Le et al., 2022; Nguyen et al., 2022). The electrochemical biosensor-based assay for the detection of tsa and htrA gene of O. tsutsugamushi was developed using AuNPs/CNF and GO modified screen-printed electrodes respectively (Kala et al., 2020a, 2021). The reported methods have drawbacks like high cost, less sensitivity and specificity.

The aim of the present study is to create a cost effective and disposable paper electrode-based point of care diagnostics for scrub typhus with better sensitivity, and specificity. The paper-based screen-printed electrodes are gaining prominence owing to their better performance and lower cost in comparison to other substrate materials (Mohanraj et al., 2020; Cao et al., 2020; Ge et al., 2015). The smaller size and ability to work with less sample volumes broaden up its application in the development of low-cost point of care systems (POCs). The working electrode surface used in the study is modified using GQDs@AuNPs nanocomposite to achieve better sensitivity, large surface area and to impart functional groups for attachment of bioreceptors (DNA probe). Gold nanoparticles-based nanocomposites with other carbon-based nanomaterials have been proved to provide a highly sensing matrix for electrochemical detection (Kala et al., 2020a, 2021; Stefanov et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2020; Verma et al., 2021). The GQDs are a zero-dimensional nanomaterial with better stability, surface reactivity, and biocompatibility. The synergistic effects of AuNPs with GQDs enhance their properties such as large surface-to-volume ratio, electrocatalytic activity and are useful in the development of electroanalytical devices (Bhardwaj et al., 2019). The GQDs@AuNPs composite is used in a few studies as a matrix for electrochemical biosensing and gaining the attention of researchers due to its excellent properties, easy and ecofriendly synthesis strategy. The present study is focused on the application of GQDs@AuNPs nanocomposite for the development of paper-based platform for DNA based sensors.

The methylene blue (MB), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC) and N-Hydroxysuccinimide (NHS) were purchased from Sigma Aldrich, USA. Potato starch was purchased from SRL and Gold (III) chloride (HAuCl4.3H2O) from Himedia. The sodium chloride, disodium phosphate (Na2HPO4), sodium dihydrogen phosphate (NaH2PO4), tris (hydroxymethyl) aminomethane (Tris), ethylenediamine-tetraaceticacid (EDTA) and other chemicals were procured from Qualigens, India. 5′-NH2 linked ssDNA probe 21 mer (5′TGGGGTCTTGGATTCTTGTGA 3’) complementary to the 47 kDa htrA gene sequence was designed using bioinformatics tools at Amity University, Haryana and synthesized from BioServe Biotechnologies (INDIA) Pvt. Ltd. The PBS buffer composition for 500 ml volume includes 0.137 M NaCl, 0.0027 M KCl, 0.01 M Na2HPO4 and 0.0018 NaH2PO4 in MilliQ water and the final pH was adjusted to 7.2. The TE buffer composition for 500 ml volume includes 10 mM Tris and 1 mM EDTA and the final pH was adjusted to 8.0. The milli Q water was used to prepare all reagents and buffer solutions.

The Palmsens Potentiostat/Galvanostat (PalmSens4) was used to carry out the electrochemical measurements. The SPPEs were purchased (Class one system S&T Pvt. Ltd., India) and modified using GQDs@AuNPs at Amity University, Haryana. The nanoparticles were characterized using UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Agilent, Cary 100), FTIR (PerkinElmer), Fluorescence spectrofluorometer (Agilent, Cary Eclipse) and HR-TEM (Hitachi, H-7500). The SPPE at different stages of fabrication was characterized using Nova Nano FE-SEM 450 (FEI) and Multimode Scanning Probe Microscope (Bruker).

The GDNA from different sources i.e., patient's blood samples, E. coli, K. pneumoniae, S. aureus, and L. interrogans were extracted using DNA isolation kit (QIA amp DNA mini kit). The quantitative and qualitative analysis of isolated GDNA samples was performed using microfluidic UV/Vis spectrophotometer (QIAxpert system). The GDNA samples were denatured at 95 °C for 5 min before hybridization to the developed DNA sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of graphene quantum dots-gold (GQDs@AuNPs) nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: The graphene quantum dots-gold nanocomposite was synthesized using the reported method [20] with slight modifications. The Graphene quantum dots were synthesized using simple starch and water by hydrothermal method. To prepare the GQDs, 1.8% starch solution (50 ml) was prepared using distilled water. The solution was stirred at 50 °C for 30 min to prepare a homogenous suspension. The suspension was further transferred to the Teflon-lined hydrothermal autoclave and incubated for 8 h at 180 °C for hydrothermal synthesis. The obtained pale-yellow solution of GQDs was centrifuged at 10,000 RPM for 30 min and the supernatant was separated. The GQDs solution was diluted using distilled water in a ratio of 1:3 in a beaker (5 ml) and stirred at RT for dispersion of suspended nanoparticles. To prepare the GQDs@AuNPs nanocomposite, 60 μl of 25 mM HAuCl4.H2O was added dropwise in GQDs solution and stirred at RT for 30 min. Further, the obtained GQDs@AuNPs nanocomposite suspension was centrifuged at 10,000 RPM for 20 min and the pellet was resuspended in MilliQ water and stored at 7–8 °C for subsequent uses.

The bare screen-printed paper electrode (BSPPE) comprised of carbon as a working and counter electrode, whereas Ag/Agcl as a reference electrode. The working electrode was modified by drop casting of 2 μl of GQDs@AuNPs nanocomposite suspension followed by incubation at 40 °C until complete drying. The electrode was washed multiple times with ethanol and water prior to the electrode modification steps. The EIS (electrochemical impedance spectroscopy) measurements were performed before and after modification of electrode surface using 2.5 mM potassium ferricyanide solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Immobilization of ssDNA probe onto the GQDs@AuNPs modified screen printed paper electrode and measurement of sensor response
ce:section.ce:para: The GQDs@AuNPs modified electrode was treated with EDAC: NHS (E: N) to functionalize the COOH group for cross linking with NH2 labeled ssDNA probe. The modified SPPEs were cleansed initially with distilled water and incubated with E: N for 2.3 h after following the drying step at RT. After the treatment of the electrode, 4 μl of 5′NH2 labeled ssDNA Probe (20 pmol) was added to the surface and incubated overnight in a humid chamber at 7–8 °C. The electrode was then washed with TE to remove the unbound DNA probe from the surface. The electrode was dried at RT and stored at 4 °C till further use.

The electrochemical response of DNA sensor with single stranded GDNA (ssGDNA) of O. tsutsugamushi (denatured at 95 °C for 5 min) was measured by cyclic voltammetry (CV) using methylene blue as a redox indictor dye. The DNA sensor was incubated with different concentrations of ssGDNA (0.0097 ng/μl-10 ng/μl) of O. tsutsugamushi and the relative peak current values were measured in a potential range of −0.4 to 0.1 V with scan rate of 30 mV/s. The sensor response was recorded before and after hybridization with ssGDNA to validate its results. The sensitivity of the DNA sensor (SD) was calculated using the formula, SD = m/A (m = slope of linear equation, A = area of working electrode). The calculated SD was further used to calculate the limit of detection (LOD) using the formula, LOD = σ/S (σ = standard deviation, S = sensitivity).

The synthesized GQDs and GQDs@AuNPs nanocomposite properties were assessed using UV–Vis, photoluminescence (PL), FTIR, and HR-TEM. The modification of bare screen-printed paper electrode (BSPPE) was also monitored using EIS, FTIR, AFM and FE-SEM. The UV–Vis spectra were recorded in a wavelength of 200–800 nm using an aqueous suspension of the GQDs and GQDs@AuNPs. The FTIR analysis of nanocomposite was also performed at frequency of 500–4000 cm−1 in order to study the functional groups and to ensure the deposition of GQDs@AuNPs nanocomposite onto the paper electrode surface (WE).

The ability of DNA sensor to discriminate between the targeted DNA sequences (GDNA of O. tsutsugamushi) with other bacterial GDNA was evaluated using CV. The CV peak current was measured before and after hybridization of DNA sensor with ssGDNA of O. tsutsugamushi, E. coli, K. pneumoniae, S. aureus, and L. interrogans to evaluate its specificity. The sensor specificity was assessed at concentration of 10 ng/μl for all DNA samples. The O. tsutsugamushi is an intracellular habitat of human blood cells, so the human genomic DNA was used as a control (negative). The patient's sample contains the huge contamination of human GDNA (h-GDNA) that creates interference in molecular diagnosis assays.

The stringency of the ssDNA probe sequence towards the htrA gene was evaluated using cDNA sequence with multiple mismatched bases (Table 1 ). The DNA sensor was hybridized with complementary DNA (cDNA), varied mismatched bases and the relative current values were recorded with respect to the ssDNA probe. The change in relative current values with respect to DNA probe was used for evaluating the stringency of the ssDNA probe towards the complementary DNA sequence and with varied number of mismatched bases.

The storage stability of was examined by monitoring the cyclic voltammetric response after every 30 days using methylene blue as a redox species. The DNA sensor stability was evaluated at a storage temperature of 4 °C and the sensor response was recorded to find out the changes in peak current values and to calculate the % loss.

The DNA sensor validation process was performed using IgM ELISA confirmed patient's DNA samples (n = 25) using cyclic voltammetry. The GDNA was extracted from the patient's blood samples and then processed (denaturation at 95 °C for 5 min) to hybridize with ssDNA probe. The hybridization event was monitored by their relative Ip values assessed from cyclic voltammogram. The difference in Ip values with respect to the probe, negative control (h-GDNA) and positive control (PCR confirmed scrub typhus DNA sample) was recorded and compared in order to validate the patient samples. A similar concentration (20 ng/μl) of all ssGDNA samples was used for hybridization with ssDNA probe. The patient samples used for validation were also confirmed using IgM ELISA in order to compare the results with the commercial kit.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of GQDs and GQDs@AuNPs nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of GQDs and GQDs@AuNPs was assured by UV–Vis, FTIR, Flourometry, FE-SEM and HRTEM. Modification of the paper electrode at each step was analyzed using FTIR, FE-SEM, AFM and EIS. The UV–Vis spectra (Fig. 1 A (a)) of GQDs exhibit a strong peak around 250 nm pertaining to a π- π* transition of aromatic sp2 domain with an extended spectrum into the visible range (Zhu et al., 2015). The GQDs successfully reduced the HAucl4.3H2O precursor salt to form Au nanoparticles, as an additional peak was seen in the spectra at around 528 nm (Fig. 1A (b)) of surface plasmon resonance (SPR). characteristic peak of gold nanoparticles. The low absorbance of AUNPs at 528 nm resulted from adsorption of GQDs on its surface and the formation of GQDs@AuNPs nanocomposite (Niu et al., 2018). The photoluminescence (PL) study of GQDs was carried out at 365 nm and an emission peak (Fig S1) was seen at 447 nm. The peak at 447 nm corresponds to the polyaromatic sp2 carbon nanostructure and surface functional groups of GQDs (Bhardwaj et al., 2019).

Further, the GQDs and GQDs@AuNPs were characterized and compared using FTIR spectroscopy. The FTIR spectrum showed an intense and broad peak (Fig. 1B (a)) at 3420 cm−1 corresponding to the OH groups of carboxylic acid and peaks at 1660, 1402 and 845 cm−1 attributed to –CO, –CC and aromatic -C-H groups of GQDs (Bhardwaj et al., 2019). The FTIR spectra of GQDs@AuNPs (Fig. 1B (b)) exhibit peaks at 3420 cm−1 due to the carboxyl group of GQDs adsorbed on AuNPs surface.

Further, GQDs and GQDs@AuNPs were characterized using HRTEM in order to analyze their size and morphology. The TEM was used to confirm the size and morphology of the GQDs@AuNPs nanocomposite. The AuNPs appear in a hexagonal shape with the maximum particles in the size range of 20–25 nm. Fig. 1C and Fig S2 show the HRTEM images of GQDs@AuNPs and GQDs respectively. The size range of the nanocomposite is useful for cell imaging and biosensing application. Further, the HRTEM images of GQDs@AuNPs show the inter plane spacing of 1.9 Å and 2.2 Å which agrees with the (200) and (111) lattice of FCC (face center cubic) gold (Botteon et al., 2021). The SAED pattern (Fig S3) of GQDs@AuNPs also confirms its polycrystalline structure with the rings that can be assigned to Au (111), (200), (220), and (311). The lattice space of (020) and (002) of the graphitic carbon on the surface of GQDS@AUNPs shows that the inner core AUNPs are stabilized by the GQDs and form a core/shell GQDs@AuNPs structure (Chen et al., 2019; Wu et al., 2012).

The comparison of the bare screen-printed paper electrode (SPPE) and GQDs@AuNPs modified electrode was done using FTIR analysis (Fig S4). The FTIR spectra of GQDs@AuNPs modified electrode shows transmittance peak at 14,012 and 1660 cm−1 that confirms the deposition of GQDs@AuNPs on the working electrode surface. No such intense transmittance peaks were observed in the bare SPPE.

The working electrode surface was characterized using AFM at different steps of modification in order to measure the parameters such as surface roughness (Rq, Ra) and height (Fig. 2 ). The surface roughness of the bare carbon electrode (Rq = 25.4 nm, Ra = 19.1 nm) was higher due to the larger voids and porous structure of carbon-based working electrode. Further, it was also confirmed from the measurement of height (78.7 nm) in 3D image of the bare electrode. The bare electrode modified with GQDs@AuNPs nanocomposite forms a homogenous layer on the electrode surface making it smooth thereby decreasing the surface roughness (Rq = 21.8 nm, Ra = 16.8 nm) and the height (60.1 nm) in 3D image of SPPE/GQDs@AuNPs. The immobilization of the DNA probe onto the modified SPPE/GQDs@AuNPs increases the surface roughness (Rq = 25.1 nm, Ra = 18.8 nm) and height (77.2 nm) due to the formation of a bimolecular layer on the surface.

Further, the SPPE/GQDs@AuNPs/DNA probe sensor was also characterized using FE-SEM in order to ensure the deposition of GQDs@AuNPs onto the electrode surface and immobilization of the DNA probe. The comparisons of paper electrode working surface area at different steps have been shown in Fig. 3 . Fig. 3a, shows the bare paper electrode with the carbon working surface area, Fig. 3b, shows the GQDs@AuNPs modified working electrode (WE), and Fig. 3c, shows the ssDNA probe immobilized GQDs@AuNPs modified WE. The micrographs of electrode fabrication steps clearly show the structural changes (Fig. 3). The images of the bare electrode show the granular carbon particles layer on working electrode surface (Fig. 3a), and after modification with GQDs@AuNPs (Fig. 3b), shiny spherical nanoparticles were appeared on the surface to ensure the deposition of nanocomposite on the working electrode. A thin film with a dense cloudy structure was visualized after the immobilization of ssDNA probe on the GQDs@AuNPs/SPPE electrode confirming the change in the surface morphology (Fig. 3c).

The DNA sensor was also characterized with electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) using 2.5 mM potassium ferricyanide in PBS buffer (pH 7.2) in a frequency range of 0.1 Hz–105 Hz with the Edc = 0.15 V and Eac = 0.006 V. The EIS (Fig. 4 ) analysis was performed to ensure the modification steps of the paper electrode and the hybridization event with the ssGDNA of O. tsutsugamushi. The Rct value of the bare electrode was high (2.9 KΩ) in comparison to the GQDs@AuNPs modified electrode (0.6 KΩ) due to the enhancement in conductivity of electrode surface by the synergetic effects of gold-graphene quantum dots nanocomposite. After covalent attachment of ssDNA Probe onto the modified paper electrode, the Rct value again raised (7.328 KΩ) due to the presence of negatively charged phosphate group of DNA which repel the [Fe (CN)6]3− ions to reach the electrode surface. The Rct value was again raised to 8.165 KΩ and 9.155 KΩ when hybridized respectively with 25 ng/μl and 50 ng/μl ssGDNA of O. tsutsugamushi due to the enumeration of phosphate group on electrode surface. The EIS studies ensure the step involved in modification of paper electrode and confirm the hybridization event on the electrode surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of DNA sensor response using cyclic voltammetry
ce:section.ce:para: The cyclic voltammetric studies of the DNA sensor shows a linear response with various tsutsugamushi ssGDNA dilutions (Fig. 5 ). The CV response was recorded before and after immobilization of htrA gene specific 5′NH2 linked ssDNA probe onto the modified electrode using 1 mM methylene blue (MB). An increase in Ip value was observed after immobilization of probe due to the addition of guanine bases (DNA) onto the electrode surface that binds with MB molecules resulting in enhanced electron transfer kinetics (Kala et al., 2020a, b; Singh et al., 2017; Rashid et al., 2022; Tang et al., 2022). A further increase in the Ip values was observed after hybridization of DNA probe with different dilutions (0.0009 ng/μl- 10 ng/μl) of ssGDNA of O. tsutsugamushi. This was attributed to an increase in the number of guanine bases on electrode surface that enhanced the electron transfer rate. The sensor detection limit (LOD) was calculated as 0.002 ng/μl from the standard calibration curve shown in inset (II) of Fig. 5. The sensor shows a linear response with various concentrations of O. tsutsugamushi ssGDNA with a correlation coefficient (R2) of 0.993. The sensitivity of the DNA sensor was calculated as 7700 μA/cm2/ng for ssGDNA of O. tsutsugamushi using cyclic voltammetry. The linear response of the sensor with the ssGDNA dilutions confirms the authenticity of hybridization events on electrode surface. The sensor response was recorded in triplicate and all values were plotted by taking average values. The limit of detection (LOD) of the DNA sensor was calculated as using the cyclic voltammetric response of DNA sensor. The results were compared with already reported methods for detection of O. tsutsugamushi in terms of sensitivity, specificity and stability. The overall performance of the developed biosensor was found to be better among all the conventional assays.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Specificity, selectivity and stability of DNA sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The developed DNA sensor is highly specific for the complimentary ssGDNA as an increase in Ip value with respect to probe was monitored only after hybridization with ssGDNA of O. tsutsugamushi (Fig. 6 ). The other bacterial GDNA samples (E. coli, K. pneumoniae, S. aureus, and L. interrogans) and negative control (h-GDNA) show inconsiderable changes in Ip values after hybridization with DNA probe. The developed DNA sensor has the ability to discriminate between the target DNA sequences with non-complementary DNA sequences, thereby making it an efficient tool to diagnose O. tsutsugamushi with higher specificity in the patient's blood samples.

The DNA probe stringency for the complementary binding site on target ssGDNA was monitored using the selectivity study. The DNA sensor shows a higher relative peak current (Ip) with respect to the probe with the cDNA sequence and decline in peak current was observed after hybridization with cDNA sequences containing different numbers of base mismatches (Fig S5). The peak current values after hybridization with multiple bases mismatched DNA was equivalent to the DNA probe values, which shows no or inconsiderable changes in current for non-complimentary DNA sequences.

The DNA sensor was found stable for six months (180 days) in a storage condition of 4 °C. The loss in sensor response was calculated as 12% with respect to the initial values, that shows a good stability of DNA sensor on storage condition of 4 °C (Fig S6).

The considerable changes in peak current (Ip) values (Fig. 7 ) were only observed in 12 patient samples and positive control with respect to the probe. This is due to the hybridization of complementary ssGDNA with DNA probe. The sensor response (Ip) in CV for the positive control (PC) and positive samples (A) with respect to probe was higher but no or inconsiderable sensor response was observed with negative control and the rest of 13 negative patient's samples (B) and TE buffer without DNA samples (C). The lower DNA response with the negative samples, NC was due to the non-complementary ssGDNA that was not able to bind with the DNA probe and wash out during the washing steps followed every hybridization event. Further, the paired T-TEST was used to evaluate the similarity between variables of data sets i.e., positive samples with the positive control and negative samples with negative control. The obtained P-values (**, P-Value≤0.05) were less than 0.05 that proves the acceptance of null hypothesis and concluded that the mean values of the variables are equal in both data sets. The samples were also confirmed using a commercial IgM ELISA kit and show compatible results with DNA sensor. The specificity of the DNA sensor is mainly contributed by the ssDNA probe sequence specific to the htrA gene which shows 100% homology with genome of O. tsutsugamushi. No homology was found with the human and other pathogens on sequence alignment (BLAST). The obtained results were also compatible with the specificity study, which shows inconsiderable sensor response towards the negative control and other bacterial species in comparison to the positive control.

The GQDs@AuNPs nanocomposite-based DNA biosensor is a novel method for detecting O. tsutsugamushi. The sensor was characterized by UV–Vis, FTIR, AFM, FESEM, HRTEM and electrochemical techniques. The sensor shows an excellent analytical performance with the LOD of 0.002 ng/μl and sensitivity of 7700 μA/cm2/ng for ssGDNA of O. tsutsugamushi. The biosensor was able to selectively determine O. tsutsugamushi in the patient's sample, thereby proving suitable for clinical applications. The developed DNA sensor has many important features such as low cost, miniaturized, easy to use, rapid response, stability and label free detection. Thus, it could be a method of choice in future for detection of O. tsutsugamushi in biological and clinical samples.
The funding to carry out the proposed work was received from the Department of Science and Technology, (DST), Govt. of India (SP/YO/079/2017).

All procedures performed in studies involving human samples were in accordance with the ethical standards of the institutional ethical committee of Maharishi Markandeshwar Medical College & Hospital (MMMC&H), Kumarhatti-Solan. Informed consent was obtained from all individual participants involved in the study.

Deepak Kala: Conceptualization, Methodology, Software, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft preparation, Data curation. Tarun Kumar Sharma: Conceptualization, Supervision. Shagun Gupta: Software, Formal analysis, Visualization. Reena Saini: Visualization, Validation. Adesh Kumar Saini; Walaa F. Alsanie: Formal analysis, Validation. Vijay Kumar Thakur: Supervision, Validation. Ankur Kaushal: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Validation, Formal analysis, Visualization, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful to Dr. Amisha Sharma, Head, Department of Microbiology, MMMC&H, Solan, H. P. for providing patient's DNA sample to carry out the research work. We would also like to thank the Central Instrument Research Facility, Amity University Haryana (UV–Vis, FTIR, and PL facility), Central Instrumentation Laboratory, Punjab University (HRTEM), and Materials research center, MNIT Jaipur (FE-SEM and AFM) for providing the characterization facilities. Walaa F. Alsanie acknowledges Taif University for support No. TURSP (2020/53).
